ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fermented braised mandarin fish. Mapped to the broad 'fish food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for fermented freshwater fish preparations is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Hui Zhou Chou Gui Yu. A00GJ (freshwater fish) is used as the closest defensible anchor. The fermentation and braising preparation steps are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific traditional Chinese fermented fish dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate regional prepared fish dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Red-Braised Fermented Mandarin Fish is approximately 300 g, representing a generous single-person main-dish portion as served in Anhui-style restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mandarin, cooked (closest approved-source equivalent); values adjusted for fermentation and braising additions including soy sauce, sugar, and pork
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hui Zhou Chou Gui Yu (Red-Braised Fermented Mandarin Fish) exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked freshwater fish (mandarin/perch-type) combined with documented contributions from the braising sauce components (soy sauce, sugar, Shaoxing wine, pork). Sodium is elevated significantly due to salt-fermentation and soy sauce. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation and are retained where they align with the USDA-anchored estimate. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.5 g/100 g based on pork and fish fat composition. Cholesterol estimated at approximately 65 mg/100 g based on fish and pork content.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised fish dish; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC — International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Red-Braised Fermented Mandarin Fish (Hui Zhou Chou Gui Yu). GI is estimated at 25 (low) based on the food's macronutrient profile: high protein (18.5 g/100 g), moderate fat (8.5 g/100 g), and low carbohydrate (3.5 g/100 g) from the braising sauce. The analogous reference is braised/cooked fish dishes with similar macro profiles documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 3.5 × 3) / 100 = 2.6 g, classified as very low. Fermentation-derived bioactive compounds may further moderate postprandial glucose response.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (650 mg/100 g; ~1,950 mg per 300 g serving) from salt-fermentation and soy sauce braising
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1,950 mg sodium — approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients; if consumed, pair with unsalted vegetables and avoid additional soy sauce or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100 g); high protein supports satiety and glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Red-Braised Fermented Mandarin Fish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low GI, low carbohydrate load (GL ~2.6 per serving), and high protein content; however, the added sugar in the braising sauce should be noted and portion size managed to limit total sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (cardiovascular risk factor) partially offset by fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids and selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (650 mg/100 g) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk and warrants strict portion control; the fish component provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids (~1.8 g/100 g) and selenium (22.5 mcg/100 g), which may offer partial cardioprotective benefit, but the overall sodium burden makes this dish unsuitable for frequent consumption in cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium and pork fat may be pro-inflammatory; fish protein and selenium may support immune function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mandarin fish provides selenium (22.5 mcg/100 g) and protein that may support immune function, but the high sodium content and saturated fat from pork may exacerbate inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; consume occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red-Braised Fermented Mandarin Fish (徽州臭鳜鱼 / 徽州臭鱖魚, Huīzhōu chòu guìyú), also known as 'Stinky Mandarin Fish' or Hui Zhou Chou Gui Yu, is the most iconic dish of Anhui (Huizhou) cuisine and one of the most celebrated regional specialties of East-Central China. The dish is prepared through a two-stage process: fresh mandarin fish (Siniperca chuatsi) is first lightly salted and allowed to ferment under controlled temperature and pressure for several days — typically 5 to 7 days — until it develops a characteristic pungent aroma reminiscent of stinky tofu, and its flesh becomes exceptionally firm and dense. The fermented fish is then pan-fried until golden and red-braised in a rich, dark sauce composed of soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, sugar, ginger, garlic, and minced pork. The result is a dish with a deeply savory, umami-rich flavor, a firm and 'snappy' texture prized by connoisseurs, and a complex aroma that is far more appealing on the palate than its scent suggests. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (18.5 g/100 g), moderate in fat (8.5 g/100 g), and low in carbohydrates (3.5 g/100 g), yielding approximately 165 kcal per 100 g. It is a meaningful source of selenium (22.5 mcg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100 g), niacin (4.5 mg/100 g), and phosphorus (180 mg/100 g). The primary clinical concern is its very high sodium content (650 mg/100 g; ~1,950 mg per 300 g serving), making it unsuitable for individuals on low-sodium diets or those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate load make it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. The dish is typically served as a main course at lunch or dinner in Anhui-style restaurants and is considered a connoisseur specialty food experience.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Red-Braised Fermented Mandarin Fish exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked freshwater fish entries combined with documented braising sauce contributions. The input AI-generated values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore rated 'medium'.
notes: 1. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific fermented and braised preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16; fermentation and braising alter moisture significantly from raw fish values. 2. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (65.0 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked fish and pork fat composition data; no direct measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 3. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (300g)' entries); the duplicate has been removed and a single 300 g default serving is used. 4. The input source cited ('Journal of Food Science', onlinelibrary.wiley.com) is not on the approved source list for primary nutrient data; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved anchor instead. The Journal of Food Science citation is retained only as contextual support for fermentation process description. 5. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. 6. FoodEx2 code A00GJ (freshwater fish) is used as the closest defensible anchor; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for this fermented braised preparation. 7. This dish is not Singaporean; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and are set to null.
